section.title: David's Census and the Altar
section.sectionRationale: The book ends where atonement is made. David sins by numbering his fighting men, apparently trusting in his military strength; a plague follows, and David, the shepherd, pleads that judgment fall on him rather than the sheep. At the LORD's word he builds an altar on the threshing floor of Araunah, refusing to offer what costs him nothing, and the plague is halted. That very threshing floor becomes the site of the temple, so the book closes at the place of sacrifice and mercy, pointing to the greater atonement to come.
section.thesis: David's proud census brings judgment, but when he confesses his sin and, as shepherd, offers himself for the sheep, God's great mercy halts the plague through sacrifice on the threshing floor that will become the temple mount, so that 2 Samuel closes at the place of costly atonement, where wrath is stayed and mercy triumphs, pointing to the cross.
section.frameNote: Three beats: David's census and his stricken conscience, verses 1 to 10; the choice of judgment and the plague, verses 11 to 17; and the altar on Araunah's threshing floor where the plague is halted, verses 18 to 25.

verses.text: Again the anger of the LORD burned against Israel, and He stirred up David against them, saying, “Go and take a census of Israel and Judah.”
So the king said to Joab the commander of his army, who was with him, “Go now throughout the tribes of Israel from Dan to Beersheba and register the troops, so that I may know their number.”
But Joab replied to the king, “May the LORD your God multiply the troops a hundred times over, and may the eyes of my lord the king see it. But why does my lord the king want to do such a thing?”

Nevertheless, the king’s word prevailed against Joab and against the commanders of the army. So Joab and the commanders of the army departed from the presence of the king to register the troops of Israel.
They crossed the Jordan and camped near Aroer, south of the town in the middle of the valley, and proceeded toward Gad and Jazer.
Then they went to Gilead and the land of Tahtim-hodshi, and on to Dan-jaan and around to Sidon.
They went toward the fortress of Tyre and all the cities of the Hivites and Canaanites. Finally, they went on to the Negev of Judah, to Beersheba.
At the end of nine months and twenty days, having gone through the whole land, they returned to Jerusalem.
And Joab reported to the king the total number of the troops. In Israel there were 800,000 men of valor who drew the sword, and in Judah there were 500,000.

After David had numbered the troops, his conscience was stricken and he said to the LORD, “I have {{chata|sinned}} greatly in what I have done. Now, O LORD, I beg You to take away the iniquity of Your servant, for I have acted very foolishly.”

units.label: The plague and the shepherd's plea
units.note: let Your hand fall upon me

verses.text: When David got up in the morning, the word of the LORD had come to Gad the prophet, David’s seer:
“Go and tell David that this is what the LORD says: ‘I am offering you three options. Choose one of them, and I will carry it out against you.’”

So Gad went and said to David, “Do you choose to endure three years of famine in your land, three months of fleeing the pursuit of your enemies, or three days of plague upon your land? Now then, think it over and decide how I should reply to Him who sent me.”
David answered Gad, “I am deeply distressed. Please, let us fall into the hand of the LORD, for His {{rachamim|mercies}} are great; but do not let me fall into the hands of men.”
So the LORD sent a plague upon Israel from that morning until the appointed time, and seventy thousand of the people from Dan to Beersheba died.

But when the angel stretched out his hand to destroy Jerusalem, the LORD relented from the calamity and said to the angel who was destroying the people, “Enough! Withdraw your hand now!” At that time the angel of the LORD was by the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.

When David saw the angel striking down the people, he said to the LORD, “Surely I, the shepherd, have sinned and acted wickedly. But these sheep, what have they done? Please, let Your hand fall upon me and my father’s house.”

units.label: The altar on the threshing floor
units.note: I will not offer what costs me nothing

verses.text: And that day Gad came to David and said to him, “Go up and build an altar to the LORD on the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite.”
So David went up at the word of Gad, just as the LORD had commanded.
When Araunah looked out and saw the king and his servants coming toward him, he went out and bowed facedown before the king.
“Why has my lord the king come to his servant?” Araunah said. “To buy your threshing floor,” David replied, “that I may build an altar to the LORD, so that the plague upon the people may be halted.”
Araunah said to David, “May my lord the king take whatever seems good to him and offer it up. Here are the oxen for a burnt offering and the threshing sledges and ox yokes for the wood.
O king, Araunah gives all these to the king.” He also said to the king, “May the LORD your God accept you.”

“No,” replied the king, “I insist on paying a price, for I will not offer to the LORD my God burnt offerings that cost me nothing.” So David bought the threshing floor and the oxen for fifty shekels of silver.
And there he built an altar to the LORD and offered burnt offerings and peace offerings. Then the LORD answered the prayers on behalf of the land, and the plague upon Israel was halted.

chata.gloss: To sin, to miss the mark, to offend against God. After the census David's conscience strikes him and he confesses, 'I have sinned greatly,' begging the LORD to take away his iniquity. As with Bathsheba, David's greatness is not that he never sinned but that, convicted, he confesses honestly and casts himself on God's mercy.

rachamim.gloss: Compassion, tender mercies, deep pity; related to the word for womb, evoking a parent's yearning love. Facing judgment, David chooses to fall into the hand of the LORD, 'for His mercies are great,' trusting God's rachamim rather than the mercilessness of men. The plague is halted precisely because the LORD relents in compassion, showing that His mercy tempers His judgment.

basic.context: The book closes with a final account of sin, judgment, and atonement. David orders a census of his fighting men, counting the swordsmen of Israel and Judah. Even Joab objects, sensing something wrong in it, but the king insists. The sin seems to be pride and misplaced trust, numbering his military strength as though his security lay in the size of his army rather than in the LORD. When the count is done, David's conscience strikes him and he confesses he has sinned greatly and acted foolishly. The prophet Gad brings three options of judgment: famine, flight before enemies, or plague. David, refusing to fall into the hands of men, chooses to fall into the hand of the LORD, trusting His great mercy. A plague sweeps Israel, killing seventy thousand, until the destroying angel reaches Jerusalem and the LORD relents, saying, 'Enough.' The angel halts at the threshing floor of Araunah the Jebusite. David, seeing the people struck down, pleads as their shepherd that the judgment fall on him and his house, not on the innocent sheep. At God's command through Gad, David goes to build an altar on Araunah's threshing floor. Araunah offers to give it freely, but David insists on paying, refusing to offer God burnt offerings that cost him nothing. He builds the altar, offers sacrifices, and the plague is halted. That threshing floor becomes the site of the temple. The chapter raises questions about pride and misplaced trust, the cost of true worship, and the mercy that halts judgment through sacrifice.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative that ends the book on the note of atonement. The census sin is understood as pride and reliance on military might rather than on God, a temptation the law had guarded against by requiring a ransom with every counting, Exodus 30. The statement that the LORD stirred David, verse 1, read alongside 1 Chronicles 21:1 that Satan incited David, teaches God's sovereignty even over the occasion of judgment without making Him the author of David's sin. The chapter is descriptive; its enduring themes are honest confession, the greatness of God's mercy, the costliness of true worship, and the halting of judgment through sacrifice at the place that becomes the temple.
basic.originalLanguages: When the census is done, David's conscience strikes and he confesses, 'I have chata, sinned, greatly,' the same honest owning of guilt as after Bathsheba. Facing judgment, he chooses to fall into the hand of the LORD 'for His rachamim, His tender mercies, are great,' a word rooted in the yearning compassion of a parent's love. Sin honestly confessed and mercy deeply trusted frame the chapter, and the plague is halted precisely because the LORD relents in that great compassion.
basic.typology: The chapter is dense with types of Christ. David, the shepherd-king, offers himself for the sheep, 'let Your hand fall upon me,' foreshadowing the Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep. The plague is halted through sacrifice on an altar, pointing to the atonement that turns away wrath. And the threshing floor of Araunah becomes the temple mount, the very place of sacrifice, and lies near where Christ Himself would be sacrificed, so that the book ends at the ground where atonement is made and points to the cross.
basic.christ: The book's closing scene overflows with Christ. David the shepherd pleads that judgment fall on him, not the sheep, a shadow of Jesus, the Good Shepherd who truly bore the judgment His sheep deserved. The plague is stayed only through sacrifice on the altar, as God's wrath against sin is finally turned away only through the sacrifice of Christ. David's refusal to offer what costs him nothing points to the infinite cost of our redemption, paid not with silver but with the precious blood of Christ. And the threshing floor becomes the temple site, the place of atoning sacrifice, whose meaning is fulfilled at Calvary, where the true and final sacrifice halts the plague of sin forever.
basic.god: God is sovereign even over the occasion of judgment, yet never the author of David's sin; He is the holy Judge whose anger against sin is real, sending the plague, and the merciful Lord who relents and says 'Enough' when the angel reaches Jerusalem. David rightly trusts God's great mercy over the mercilessness of men. Above all, God is the one who provides the place and means of atonement, halting judgment through sacrifice, revealing a God whose justice and mercy meet at the altar, and pointing to the cross where they meet perfectly.
basic.crossReferences: First Chronicles 21 is the parallel account, adding that Satan incited David and identifying the site as the temple mount. Exodus 30 required a ransom whenever Israel was counted, illuminating the census sin. John 10 presents Christ as the Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep. And Second Chronicles 3 identifies Araunah's threshing floor as the site of Solomon's temple.
basic.oneStory: The book ends where it must, at the place of atonement. David's proud census brings judgment, but the shepherd-king offers himself for the sheep, God's great mercy relents, and the plague is halted through sacrifice on the threshing floor that becomes the temple. This closing scene of wrath turned away through costly sacrifice points forward to Jesus, the Good Shepherd who bears His people's judgment and, by His own precious blood, halts the plague of sin forever.

context.literary: This final chapter pairs with chapter 21, the two accounts of national judgment ended by atonement forming the outer frame of the appendix. It ends 2 Samuel not with David's death, which comes in 1 Kings, but at an altar, deliberately closing the book on the theme of sacrifice and mercy. The purchase of the threshing floor sets up the temple, linking David's reign to the sanctuary his son will build. David's plea as shepherd echoes his origins and his whole calling.
context.historical: A military census was a way of assessing strength for war, and doing it apparently signaled reliance on numbers rather than on God; the law required a ransom at every counting to acknowledge the people belonged to God. Threshing floors were flat, elevated, open places, and Araunah's, on the hill north of the old city, became the site of Solomon's temple. Refusing a free gift and insisting on payment reflected David's conviction that true sacrifice must cost the worshiper something.
context.canonical: The chapter's placement at the end of 2 Samuel, and its identification in Chronicles as the temple site, make it a hinge toward the temple and the whole sacrificial system. The shepherd offering himself for the sheep, the halting of judgment through sacrifice, and the costly offering all feed into the Bible's theology of atonement, fulfilled in Christ. The tension between 'the LORD stirred David' and 'Satan incited David' has shaped Christian reflection on divine sovereignty and human responsibility.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To close 2 Samuel with an account of sin, judgment, and atonement, showing David's proud census answered by plague, his shepherd's plea for the sheep, and the halting of judgment through costly sacrifice on the threshing floor that becomes the temple, so ending the book at the place of atonement and pointing to the sacrifice yet to come.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific sin, judgment, and atonement, not a rule about censuses or a formula for averting plagues; the sin lay in the pride and misplaced trust behind the counting. What endures is the pattern of honest confession, trust in God's great mercy, the truth that true worship costs the worshiper, and the halting of judgment through sacrifice, all pointing to Christ.

debates.question: Who incited David to take the census, since 2 Samuel says the LORD and 1 Chronicles says Satan?

views.label: God's sovereign overruling working through a real tempter
views.case: Second Samuel 24:1 says the LORD, whose anger burned against Israel, stirred David, while 1 Chronicles 21:1 says Satan incited him; Scripture holds these together as it does with Job, where Satan acts only within God's sovereign permission; God, righteously angry with Israel, sovereignly ordained the occasion of judgment and permitted Satan to tempt David, yet David remains fully responsible, confessing 'I have sinned greatly,' so God's sovereignty and Satan's real agency and David's guilt all stand together without making God the author of sin.

views.label: Complementary perspectives on one event
views.case: Others stress that the two accounts give different vantage points on the same event, the ultimate divine purpose and the immediate satanic instigation, much as the cross was both God's set plan and the work of wicked hands, Acts 2:23; on this reading the texts are not in conflict but layered, teaching that God can use even a tempter's assault to accomplish His righteous ends while the human agent bears the guilt of his own choice.

debates.bearing: Both readings uphold God's sovereignty, Satan's genuine but permitted agency, and David's full moral responsibility, guarding against the error of making God the author of evil while affirming that nothing, not even judgment through a tempter, lies outside God's rule, a mystery finally illumined at the cross.

study.originalLanguages: When the counting is finished, David's conscience strikes him and he confesses in the plainest terms, 'I have chata, sinned, greatly in what I have done,' begging the LORD to take away his iniquity, the same honest owning of guilt that marked him after Bathsheba. His greatness lies not in never sinning but in this readiness to confess and cast himself on God. Then, facing three forms of judgment, David chooses to fall into the hand of the LORD 'for His rachamim are great,' verse 14. The word rachamim means tender mercies, deep compassion, and is rooted in the Hebrew word for the womb, evoking the yearning, visceral love of a parent for a child; David would rather be handled by that fierce compassion than by the mercilessness of men. And the chapter proves him right: when the destroying angel reaches Jerusalem, the LORD relents in that very compassion, 'Enough! Withdraw your hand,' and the plague is halted at the altar. Sin honestly confessed and divine rachamim deeply trusted frame the book's final scene, where mercy triumphs over judgment through sacrifice.

typology.type: The shepherd-king offering himself for the sheep, 'let Your hand fall upon me'
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep and bears the judgment they deserved, John 10:11 and Isaiah 53:6.

typology.type: Judgment halted through sacrifice on the altar
typology.fulfillment: Christ's sacrifice, which turns away God's wrath against sin once for all, Romans 3:25 and Hebrews 10:12.

typology.type: The threshing floor that becomes the temple, the place of atonement
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true temple and true sacrifice, in whom atonement is finally made, John 2:19-21 and Hebrews 9:11-12.

study.christ: The book's closing scene overflows with Christ. David the shepherd-king pleads that the judgment fall on him and not the sheep, 'these sheep, what have they done?', a shadow of Jesus, the Good Shepherd who truly laid down His life for the sheep and on whom the LORD laid the iniquity of us all, John 10:11 and Isaiah 53:6. The plague is stayed only through sacrifice on the altar, foreshadowing the truth that God's wrath against sin is turned away only through the sacrifice of Christ, set forth as the propitiation for our sins, Romans 3:25. David's refusal to offer what costs him nothing points to the infinite cost of our redemption, purchased not with silver but with the precious blood of Christ, 1 Peter 1:18-19. And the threshing floor of Araunah becomes the temple mount, the very place of atoning sacrifice, near where Christ Himself would be offered, so that 2 Samuel ends at the ground of atonement and points straight to Calvary, where the true and final sacrifice halts the plague of sin forever and God's justice and mercy meet in perfect embrace.
study.god: God is sovereign even over the occasion of judgment, His anger against Israel's sin real and His purposes ruling over the whole event, yet He is never the author of David's sin, which David freely owns. He is the holy Judge whose wrath against sin is not pretended, sending a plague that kills seventy thousand, and at the same time the merciful Lord who relents the moment the angel reaches Jerusalem, crying 'Enough!' David rightly trusts that God's mercies are great and would rather fall into His hand than into the hands of men, and the chapter vindicates that trust. Above all, God is the one who provides the place and the means of atonement, halting judgment through sacrifice on the threshing floor that becomes His temple, revealing a God in whom justice and mercy meet at the altar, a meeting perfected at the cross where He is both just and the justifier of those who trust in Jesus.

crossReferences.note: The parallel account, adding that Satan incited David and that Araunah's threshing floor is the site where the temple would be built.
The law requiring a ransom for each person whenever Israel was counted, illuminating why the census incurred guilt.
Jesus, the Good Shepherd who lays down His life for the sheep, fulfilling David's plea to bear the judgment for his people.
Solomon built the temple on Mount Moriah at the threshing floor of Araunah, the very place of David's atoning altar.

study.oneStory: The book ends where the whole story has been heading, at the place of atonement. David's proud census brings judgment, but the shepherd-king offers himself for the sheep, God's great mercy relents at the cry of 'Enough,' and the plague is halted through costly sacrifice on the threshing floor that becomes the temple mount. This closing scene of wrath turned away through sacrifice gathers up the deepest themes of 2 Samuel, the honest confession of sin, the greatness of God's mercy, and the need for atonement, and points forward to Jesus, the Good Shepherd and Son of David who bears His people's judgment and, by His own precious blood offered on a hill near this very place, halts the plague of sin forever. So the book of the flawed but forgiven king closes at an altar, pointing to the cross.

quiz.q: What was sinful about David's census of the fighting men?

options.text: It reflected pride and misplaced trust in his military strength rather than in the LORD.
options.note: The sin lay in relying on numbers and might rather than on God, which even Joab sensed was wrong.

options.text: Counting people was always forbidden under any circumstances.
options.note: Counting itself was not absolutely forbidden; the law allowed it with a ransom, and the sin here was the pride and misplaced trust behind it.

options.text: It showed David trusting in his army instead of in God.
options.note: True, but this option omits the element of pride and that the law required a ransom at every counting, which was neglected.

options.text: David was jealous of Joab's command of the army.
options.note: The issue was David's reliance on numbers, not jealousy of Joab, who actually objected to the census.

quiz.q: Why does David choose to fall into the hand of the LORD rather than into the hands of men?

options.text: Because the LORD's mercies are great, so he trusts God's compassion over the mercilessness of men.
options.note: Verse 14 records David's trust in God's great mercy in choosing his judgment.

options.text: Because he thought God's judgment would be the least painful option.
options.note: David's reason is God's great mercy, not that the plague would hurt least; the plague was severe.

options.text: Because he preferred to trust God's compassion rather than human cruelty.
options.note: True, but this option omits the specific reason he gives, that the LORD's mercies are great.

options.text: Because Gad the prophet told him which option to choose.
options.note: Gad presented the options; David himself chose, trusting God's mercy, not on Gad's instruction.

quiz.q: What does David plead when he sees the plague striking the people?

options.text: That the judgment fall on him and his house, since he the shepherd sinned, not the innocent sheep.
options.note: Verse 17 records David's shepherd's plea to bear the judgment for his people.

options.text: That God spare him personally while judging the guilty people.
options.note: The opposite; David asks that the judgment fall on him, not the people.

options.text: That God's hand fall on him rather than on the people.
options.note: True, but this option omits the tender shepherd-and-sheep imagery that makes the plea a foreshadowing of Christ.

options.text: That Joab be punished for carrying out the census.
options.note: David takes the blame himself as the shepherd; he does not shift it to Joab, who had objected.

quiz.q: Why does David insist on paying Araunah for the threshing floor and oxen?

options.text: Because he will not offer the LORD burnt offerings that cost him nothing.
options.note: Verse 24 records David's refusal to offer a costless sacrifice.

options.text: Because Araunah demanded a high price for his property.
options.note: Araunah offered it all for free; it was David who insisted on paying.

options.text: Because true worship should cost the worshiper something.
options.note: True, but this option generalizes David's specific words about not offering what costs him nothing.

options.text: Because the law required the king to buy any altar site.
options.note: No such law is cited; David acts from conviction that his offering must be costly, not from a legal requirement.

quiz.q: How does the book's closing scene point to Christ?

options.text: The shepherd offers himself for the sheep and judgment is halted through costly sacrifice on the future temple site, foreshadowing the cross.
options.note: The shepherd's plea, the atoning sacrifice, and the temple site all point to Christ, John 10:11 and Romans 3:25.

options.text: It teaches that plagues can always be stopped by building altars.
options.note: The point is atonement pointing to Christ, not a formula for stopping plagues.

options.text: It shows judgment turned away through sacrifice, fulfilled at Calvary.
options.note: True, but this option omits the shepherd offering himself for the sheep, a striking foreshadowing of Christ.

options.text: It proves that David earned forgiveness by his generous payment.
options.note: The plague is halted by God's mercy through sacrifice, not by David earning forgiveness with money.

questions: David's census sprang from trusting in his own strength and numbers. Where are you tempted to find your security in resources, achievements, or influence rather than in God?
When convicted, David honestly confessed, 'I have sinned greatly.' How quick are you to own your sin plainly before God rather than excusing or minimizing it?
David chose to fall into the hand of the LORD because His mercies are great. How does trusting in the depth of God's compassion change the way you face the consequences of your failures?
David refused to offer God anything that cost him nothing. Where might your worship or service to God have become convenient and costless, and what would costly devotion look like?

The shepherd-king offered himself for the sheep, foreshadowing Christ who truly bore our judgment. How does it move you that the Good Shepherd laid down His life so the plague of sin could be halted for you forever?

translationNotes.note: That 'the LORD stirred up David' is complemented by 1 Chronicles 21:1, 'Satan incited David'; Scripture holds God's sovereign overruling and Satan's real, permitted agency together, as in Job, without making God the author of David's sin, which David fully owns.

The BSB reads 'three years of famine' following the Septuagint and 1 Chronicles 21:12, while the Hebrew here reads 'seven years'; the three-year reading matches the parallel and the threefold pattern of the options.

The BSB footnote notes that Araunah is called Ornan in 1 Chronicles 21:15; his threshing floor becomes the site of Solomon's temple on Mount Moriah, 2 Chronicles 3:1, so the book ends at the place of atonement.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Samuel 24:1-25. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter; none introduced in this file. This final chapter closes 2 Samuel at the altar on the threshing floor that becomes the temple site, the place of atonement pointing to Christ.
